prompt: ASHRAE CHD certification validates competency in:

options.text: HVAC system design including load calculations, equipment selection, and distribution design
Only plumbing fixture counts without load analysis
Electrical one-line drafting without demand study
Land surveying legal descriptions exclusively

explanation: CHD tests practical HVAC design knowledge per the CHD exam blueprint.

distractorExplanations.b: CHD requires engineering analysis of HVAC loads and systems.
distractorExplanations.c: Electrical design is a separate discipline.
distractorExplanations.d: Surveying is unrelated to CHD HVAC design.

prompt: HBDP (High-Performance Building Design Professional) emphasizes:

options.text: Integrated design delivering energy efficiency, IAQ, and sustainability across disciplines
Single-discipline silo design without coordination
Only lowest first cost without performance metrics
Exclusive focus on decorative finishes

explanation: HBDP spans architecture, mechanical, electrical, and controls integration.

distractorExplanations.b: HBDP focuses on integrated high-performance outcomes.
distractorExplanations.c: High-performance design balances lifecycle performance.
distractorExplanations.d: HBDP addresses building systems and performance integration.

prompt: Sensible cooling load calculations account for:

options.text: Conduction, solar gains, internal heat sources, and ventilation affecting dry-bulb temperature
Only latent moisture without temperature change
Structural footing weights exclusively
Only occupant latent perspiration

explanation: Sensible loads size cooling capacity and airflow for temperature control.

distractorExplanations.b: Sensible loads change dry-bulb temperature.
distractorExplanations.c: Structural weight is not a cooling load component.
distractorExplanations.d: Latent load is separate from sensible cooling load.

prompt: Latent cooling load is primarily driven by:

options.text: Moisture addition from occupants, ventilation, and infiltration requiring dehumidification
Only solar gain on opaque walls
Steel beam modulus of elasticity
Fan motor sensible heat only

explanation: Latent loads determine reheat, dehumidification, and coil selection.

distractorExplanations.b: Opaque solar gain is mostly sensible.
distractorExplanations.c: Structural material properties are not latent loads.
distractorExplanations.d: Fan heat is sensible; latent load involves moisture.

prompt: The CHD/HBDP published passing score is approximately:

options.text: 68 correct out of 100 scored items
70 correct out of 100 scored items (verify current FAQs)
83 correct out of 120 scored items
45 correct out of 60 scored items

explanation: CHD and HBDP share a similar pass threshold per ASHRAE FAQs.

distractorExplanations.c: 83/120 is the BCxP pass point.
distractorExplanations.d: CHD/HBDP use 100 scored items.

prompt: Psychrometric analysis in HVAC design is used to:

options.text: Relate dry-bulb, wet-bulb, humidity ratio, and enthalpy for process and coil selection
Size structural columns only
Calculate parking stall counts
Determine soil bearing capacity

explanation: Designers use psychrometric charts for cooling, heating, and humidification processes.

distractorExplanations.b: Psychrometrics addresses air property relationships.
distractorExplanations.c: Parking counts are unrelated to psychrometrics.
distractorExplanations.d: Geotechnical analysis is separate from HVAC psychrometrics.

prompt: ASHRAE Standard 62.1 sets:

options.text: Minimum ventilation rates and IAQ procedures for commercial and institutional buildings
Only chiller refrigerant charge limits
Structural wind load combinations exclusively
Kitchen exhaust hood fabrication welding only

explanation: Ventilation rate procedure and IAQ procedure paths are core 62.1 content.

distractorExplanations.b: Refrigerant rules are in safety and equipment standards.
distractorExplanations.c: Structural wind loads are in ASCE 7, not 62.1.
distractorExplanations.d: 62.1 addresses ventilation rates and IAQ.

prompt: VAV system design must address:

options.text: Minimum ventilation at part load, static pressure control, and reheat/dehumidification implications
Only constant maximum airflow at all times
Elimination of all outdoor air requirements
No duct sizing because velocity is irrelevant

explanation: VAV designs balance comfort, ventilation, and fan energy at part load.

distractorExplanations.b: VAV modulates airflow to match load.
distractorExplanations.c: Minimum ventilation must be maintained per 62.1.
distractorExplanations.d: Duct design and static pressure are critical VAV design tasks.

prompt: Air-side economizer design per ASHRAE 90.1 requires:

options.text: Evaluating climate zone thresholds, high-limit shutoffs, and integration with mechanical cooling
Economizers in all buildings regardless of size and climate without exception
Disabling relief air paths
No controls integration with dampers

explanation: Economizer requirements depend on climate zone and cooling system capacity.

distractorExplanations.b: 90.1 has capacity and climate exceptions.
distractorExplanations.c: Relief or return fans manage building pressurization with economizers.
distractorExplanations.d: Economizer dampers must be controlled and interlocked.

prompt: Chiller plant design selection considers:

options.text: Load profile, redundancy, kW/ton efficiency, condenser water temperatures, and part-load performance
Only chiller footprint color
A single chiller size with no part-load analysis
Ignoring cooling tower approach temperature

explanation: Plant designers match staging and sizes to load duration curves.

distractorExplanations.b: Equipment aesthetics do not drive plant selection.
distractorExplanations.c: Part-load efficiency strongly affects annual energy.
distractorExplanations.d: Tower performance affects chiller efficiency.

prompt: Duct friction loss calculations use:

options.text: Darcy-Weisbach or equivalent methods with roughness, fitting losses, and velocity limits
Only occupant count times floor area
Random duct sizes without velocity checks
Structural live load factors from ASCE 7

explanation: Total static pressure determines fan selection in distribution design.

distractorExplanations.b: Duct loss calculations use hydraulic principles.
distractorExplanations.c: Velocity and noise limits constrain duct sizing.
distractorExplanations.d: Structural live loads do not size duct friction.

prompt: Hydronic pipe sizing balances:

options.text: Flow requirements, velocity limits, pressure drop, and pumping energy
Only pipe color coding standards
Maximum velocity with no regard for erosion or noise
Elimination of all balancing valves

explanation: Oversized pipes waste capital; undersized pipes waste pump energy and cause noise.

distractorExplanations.b: Hydronic sizing is a hydraulic engineering task.
distractorExplanations.c: Velocity limits protect pipes and valves.
distractorExplanations.d: Balancing devices are required for proper distribution.

prompt: Dedicated outdoor air systems (DOAS) separate:

options.text: Ventilation and dehumidification from sensible cooling at terminal units
All heating from any ventilation airflow
Only plumbing fixtures from electrical panels
Structural steel from concrete exclusively

explanation: DOAS improves humidity control in high-performance buildings.

distractorExplanations.b: DOAS handles ventilation/latent loads; terminals handle sensible loads.
distractorExplanations.c: DOAS is an HVAC ventilation strategy.
distractorExplanations.d: Structural materials are unrelated to DOAS.

prompt: Energy recovery ventilators in design should be evaluated for:

options.text: Effectiveness, cross-leakage, frost control, and fan power impact on total ventilation energy
Only decorative grille finishes
Infinite effectiveness without manufacturer data
Eliminating all outdoor air requirements

explanation: ERV/HRV selection balances IAQ and energy in cold and hot climates.

distractorExplanations.b: Recovery performance metrics drive energy savings.
distractorExplanations.c: Designers use tested effectiveness ratings.
distractorExplanations.d: ERVs precondition OA but do not remove ventilation need.

prompt: Integrated design charrettes in HBDP projects:

options.text: Align disciplines early on load reduction, system selection, and performance targets
Occur only after construction is complete
Exclude owners and operators from participation
Focus solely on furniture procurement

explanation: Early integration prevents costly late redesign of systems.

distractorExplanations.b: Charrettes are early design collaboration tools.
distractorExplanations.c: Owners and operators inform requirements.
distractorExplanations.d: Charrettes target building performance strategies.

prompt: Load calculations for perimeter zones must consider:

options.text: Orientation-specific solar gains, conduction, and infiltration distinct from interior zones
Identical loads on all facades regardless of glazing
Only interior carpet R-value
No difference between winter and summer loads

explanation: Perimeter vs interior zoning affects system selection and controls.

distractorExplanations.b: Orientation and fenestration vary perimeter loads.
distractorExplanations.c: Carpet has minor effect vs glazing and wall assembly.
distractorExplanations.d: Heating and cooling loads vary seasonally.

prompt: Fan coil unit selection requires:

options.text: Matching coil capacity, airflow, condensate management, and noise criteria for the zone
Only selecting the smallest cabinet for aesthetics
Ignoring condensate drain slope and traps
No acoustic analysis in any occupancy

explanation: FCU design ties hydronic or refrigerant supplies to zone loads.

distractorExplanations.b: Capacity and airflow must meet peak and ventilation needs.
distractorExplanations.c: Condensate design prevents water damage and IAQ issues.
distractorExplanations.d: Noise criteria matter in hotels, offices, and healthcare.

prompt: Heat pump system design in commercial buildings should address:

options.text: Defrost, backup heat, simultaneous heating/cooling needs, and refrigerant piping limits
Only constant COP at all outdoor temperatures
Unlimited refrigerant line length without manufacturer guidance
Elimination of all ventilation loads

explanation: VRF/VRV and packaged heat pumps need careful application engineering.

distractorExplanations.b: Performance varies with outdoor conditions.
distractorExplanations.c: Piping limits affect oil return and capacity.
distractorExplanations.d: Ventilation loads remain in heat pump designs.

prompt: Healthcare HVAC design (HFDP overlap) prioritizes:

options.text: Infection control airflow, pressure relationships, filtration, and redundancy
Only lowest first cost with no pressure mapping
Eliminating all outdoor air to save energy
Negative pressure in all spaces universally

explanation: HBDP/CHD candidates should understand healthcare differs from standard office design.

distractorExplanations.b: Healthcare requires pressure and filtration control.
distractorExplanations.c: Healthcare ventilation rates are stringent.
distractorExplanations.d: Pressure relationships vary by room type.

prompt: UFAD (underfloor air distribution) design considerations include:

options.text: Swirl diffusers, slab height, cable pathways, and stratification effects on comfort
Only traditional ceiling supply with no floor plenum
Ignoring air temperature stratification
No coordination with raised access floor structure

explanation: UFAD can improve comfort and flexibility in suitable building types.

distractorExplanations.b: UFAD uses underfloor plenums and floor diffusers.
distractorExplanations.c: Stratification affects comfort and load calculations.
distractorExplanations.d: Plenum height and structure are coordinated design tasks.

prompt: Cooling tower selection depends on:

options.text: Heat rejection load, approach, wet-bulb design conditions, drift, and water treatment requirements
Only tower fan blade color
Ignoring wet-bulb temperature for the site
Assuming towers need no makeup water

explanation: Tower performance sets minimum condenser water temperature for chillers.

distractorExplanations.b: Thermal performance and water management drive selection.
distractorExplanations.c: Wet-bulb defines tower capacity and approach.
distractorExplanations.d: Evaporation requires makeup water and bleed.

prompt: Boiler plant design for heating should evaluate:

options.text: Efficiency turndown, redundancy, venting, combustion air, and distribution temperature strategy
Only boiler paint finish
Oversizing without part-load efficiency review
Eliminating all combustion air requirements

explanation: Condensing boilers need low return temperatures to achieve rated efficiency.

distractorExplanations.b: Efficiency and safety drive boiler selection.
distractorExplanations.c: Cycling at low load reduces efficiency.
distractorExplanations.d: Combustion air is code-mandated.

prompt: ASHRAE Guideline 36 sequences of operation aim to:

options.text: Standardize high-performance control sequences for common HVAC equipment
Replace all mechanical equipment sizing rules
Eliminate need for any BAS programming
Define only decorative lighting scenes

explanation: Designers reference Guideline 36 for efficient stable control strategies.

distractorExplanations.b: Guideline 36 addresses controls sequences, not equipment sizing.
distractorExplanations.c: Sequences must still be implemented in the BAS.
distractorExplanations.d: Guideline 36 covers HVAC system control logic.

prompt: Selecting supply air temperature in cooling design trades off:

options.text: Duct size, airflow rate, dehumidification capability, and terminal device performance
Only structural column spacing
Higher SAT always reduces duct size without dehumidification impact
No relationship to space humidity control

explanation: Lower SAT improves dehumidification but increases duct and fan sizing.

distractorExplanations.b: Supply air temperature affects HVAC distribution design.
distractorExplanations.c: Warmer SAT may require more airflow for dehumidification.
distractorExplanations.d: SAT is central to latent capacity delivery.

prompt: Natural ventilation hybrid design requires:

options.text: Defining operable window controls, wind/rain limits, and backup mechanical ventilation
Sealing all windows permanently
No analysis of outdoor air quality or noise
Eliminating all mechanical cooling globally

explanation: High-performance designs may use mixed-mode ventilation where climate allows.

distractorExplanations.b: Natural ventilation requires controlled operable openings.
distractorExplanations.c: Ambient conditions affect natural ventilation feasibility.
distractorExplanations.d: Hybrids combine natural and mechanical systems.
